text: Towards an AAS Event Mechanism: A proposal to extend the AAS specification Melanie Stolze Automation and Digitalisation Institute for Automation and Communication (ifak e.V.) Magdeburg, Germany 0009-0008-3259-1614 Niklas Ritz Automation and Digitalisation ifak e.V. Magdeburg, Germany 0009-0002-4712-6762 Alexander Belyaev Automation and Digitalisation ifak e.V. Magdeburg, Germany alexander.belyaev@ifak.eu Rene-Pascal Fischer Digital Twin Engineering Fraunhofer IESE Kaiserslautern, Germany 0000-0001-8261-2761 Christian Kosel Arena 2036 e.V. Stuttgart, Germany christian.kosel@arena2036.de Raghavendra Ungarala Faculty for Mechanical Engineering and Mechatronics University of Applied Sciences Karlsruhe Karlsruhe, Germany raghavendra.ungarala@h-ka.de Abstract—This work contributes to advancing Asset Ad- ministration Shell (AAS) standardization by bridging gaps in current specifications, offering a robust foundation for future industrial applications. It addresses the current experimental specification of the AAS Event element within industrial digital twin ecosystems. The existing AAS metamodel defines Event elements, but the usage is not wide spread in practice due to the experimental status of the Event element specification. To support the transition to an accepted Event element specification, this paper analyzed the current specification and proposed a redefinition of the AAS metamodel as it does not cover every use case. In this redefinition the AAS Event element is no longer a submodel element but a standalone component within the metamodel. To overcome different types of Event elements the authors also propose a scheme to build semantic identifiers for it. To implement the newly defined AAS Event element, a concept for an extended infrastructure is developed, which is based on the Eclipse BaSyx TM1 Framework. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell, Digital Twin, Events, Eventing, Event Modeling I. INTRODUCTION As digitalization reshapes engineering and production, the need for standardized information models to streamline com- plex system interactions becomes increasingly critical. The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) provides a specification framework for creating and managing information models tai- lored to describe various aspects of assets, such as their proper- ties, functionalities, or operational contexts. These information models serve specific use cases, enabling seamless integration and interoperability across digital twin environments [1]. To facilitate this, the AAS metamodel defines a standardized Representational State Transfer (REST) based interface, as outlined in Part 2 of the AAS specification series [2], which This work was funded in parts by the project ”Asset Administration Shell for the wiring harness” (VWS4LS) under contract 13IKOOSA and by the project ”DAVID” under contract 13MX006, funded by the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action, Germany. 1Eclipse Basyx is a trademark of Eclipse Foundation, Inc. allows applications to have seamless access to this structured and standardized information of digital twins. In this archi- tecture, the AAS functions as a server, while the connected applications operate as clients. This setup ensures consistent access and management of asset-related data. However, there is a growing demand for asynchronous communication mechanisms in industrial applications. Several use cases require the ability to send spontaneous and unso- licited notifications [3], [4]. Challenges become tangible in collaborative engineering settings, where product development spans multiple companies, disciplines, and toolchains [5]. Each partner maintains a decentralized, partial representation of the same asset. As a result, information is often duplicated or, at the very least, logically dependent across models. A seemingly trivial change, such as adjusting a tolerance value in one partner’s CAD submodel, can therefore require immediate recalculation of derived parameters in another partner’s simu- lation model or bill of materials. If such updates are not passed on automatically and reliably, the data becomes inconsistent, which can lead to errors whose causes are difficult to track down later and which are expensive to correct. Events hold significance not only in visionary applications but also in practical use cases. For example, a customer-facing order tracking service must surface real-time status changes, e.g. ’Your product has entered surface finishing’, as the item progresses through production. Likewise, during the life cycle of an asset, multiple digital twins may be hosted by different stakeholders (OEM, operator, service provider); these twins have to stay synchronized even though they reside in separate systems and domains. This event-driven mechanism ensures, that all parties observe the same ground truth at the moment it changes, enabling timely analytics, compliance checks, and adaptive control. In general, it is assumed in the literature that the infor- mational value of data decreases as a function of time. The longer the delay between data generation and its utilization, the lower its relevance and potential impact on operational or strategic decisions. The so-called ”time-value of data” curve illustrates this: when data is available with minimal delay, it enables predictive insights, proactive responses, and adaptive systems. In contrast, historical data, while useful for trend analysis and machine learning, only reflects the past state without capturing the dynamic behavior of the system. This observation motivates a shift in the design of data-driven systems towards architectures that support the timely detection and processing of discrete events and the shifts between life cycle phases of an asset [6]. Architectures based on event- driven principles are therefore increasingly being explored as a foundation for adaptive and responsive digital systems [7]. Within the context of the AAS, such mechanisms would be both relevant and beneficial. The current AAS metamodel provides the foundation for such interactions through its BasicEventElement. Although specified in the metamodel [8], its definition and application are currently considered experimental and not yet finalized. The recently published white paper by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA) attempts to address this gap by interpreting and explaining the Event element and different modeling approaches, supplemented with practical examples [9]. However, there are scenarios, some of which are mentioned in the AAS metamodel specification, that are not yet fully explained in terms of how they should be modeled and implemented in practice. These include, for example, events related to the existence and life cycle of AAS instances them- selves, as well as events such as security violations, alarms, system boot, or shutdown procedures. In order to enable subscriptions to such events, two essential questions arise from a modeling perspective: how can the subscription to such events be represented within the AAS framework, and where and how can an appropriate Event element be defined that observes these occurrences? This paper extends the existing explanations on AAS events provided in the mentioned white paper [9] and discusses possible approaches to address these questions. In some cases, this may require adjustments to the specification of the attributes of the BasicEventElement and may involve design decisions concerning the integration of additional infrastructural components. II. INTRODUCTION TO AAS EVENT ELEMENT The following subsections introduce the current specifica- tion of the classes BasicEventElement and EventPayload of the specified AAS Event element [8]. A. Specification of the AAS Event Element The AAS Event element, defined in [8], allows to model events and notifications, which enables external applications to subscribe and publish notifications in an AAS and its surrounding environment. At its core, the event mechanism relies on the BasicEventElement (see Fig. 1) to define Event elements and the EventPayload (see Fig. 2) to transmit event data. Fig. 1. Current Specification of the BasicEventElement [8] Fig. 2. Current Specification of the EventPayload [8] Because both classes inherit from SubmodelElement, their use is implicitly restricted to attachment within individual submodels; the implications of this limitation will be analyzed in Sec. II-B. As detailed in [9], the BasicEventElement enables the subscription and publication to a specific element of an AAS or its surrounding environment. By configuring its attributes, one can identify the element to be observed, specify the event type to be reported, set the notification frequency, and designate the message broker. The broker may be an event routing component that forwards the EventPayload from the publishing application to the subscribing application. The second element in the AAS metamodel that supports the event mechanism is the EventPayload. It defines the structure of the message that is transmitted. The payload references the AAS element under observation, identifies the concrete element that changed, states the nature of the change, specifies the publisher, and carries the event-specific data. A detailed explanation and modeling examples are provided in [9]. B. Integration of the Event Element into the AAS After introducing the Event element, the next consideration is determining the optimal placement of a BasicEventElement within an AAS environment to observe specific elements effec- tively. Although the metamodel does not prescribe a specific location, the white paper [9] outlines two plausible interpre- tations that follow from the fact that the BasicEventElement is defined as a SubmodelElement. In the first interpretation, the BasicEventElement is placed in the same submodel that contains the element to be monitored (see Fig. 3). To demon- strate this modeling strategy, Chapter 5 of the white paper Fig. 3. Approach to model a BasicEventElement in the belonging submodel (based on [9]) [9] presents a practical scenario within the Product Change Notification (PCN) submodel. In this case, an Event element named PCNEventsOutgoing is modeled directly within the PCN submodel. It observes the Records element, defined as a SubmodelElementList containing multiple product change notification records. The BasicEventElement is configured to trigger any update to the Records list such as the creation of a new PCN entry. The second interpretation groups all BasicEventElements of an AAS in a dedicated submodel, which can be named Events, as shown in Fig. 4. This arrangement also supports Event elements that reference AAS metadata and concept descriptions. A hybrid approach is also feasible: the separate Events submodel hosts those BasicEventElements that refer to AAS-wide metadata and concept descriptions, while Event elements concerned with ordinary submodel content remain embedded in their respective submodels. A practical scenario of this modeling approach also presented in Chapter 5 of the white paper [9] demonstrates, how the complete procurement process is modeled using an event-driven architecture in com- bination with the Product Request Notification (PRN), Product Request Response (PRR) and Product Order Creation (POC) submodels. In this scenario, a dedicated Event submodel is defined to encapsulate BasicEventElements that observe the instantiation of new submodels. These Event elements are configured to automatically trigger corresponding process actions upon submodel creation. III. USE CASES AND REQUIREMENTS The following section examines what can actually be sub- scribed to or published within an AAS and its surrounding environment. The Figure 5 provides a structured overview of potential types of events that may occur in the context of the AAS. It is essential to distinguish between two fundamental categories: • Events that occur within an existing AAS, such as update, creation and deletion of submodels, submodel elements, concept descriptions, AAS metadata, and submodel meta- data. Fig. 4. Approach to model all BasicEventElements in a separate submodel, e.g., called ”Events” (based on [9]) • Events related to the existence and life cycle of the AAS itself, including the creation of a new AAS instance and the deletion of an existing AAS. The specification also mentions the notification of the infrastructure-related events that occur outside any partic- ular AAS such as alarms, security incidents (e.g. logging events, access violations, role-and-rights mismatches, denial- of-service attempts), and life cycle events of infrastructure components (e.g., booting, shutdown, out-of-memory condi- tions) [8]. As explained in the previous section, the current metamodel defines the BasicEventElement as a SubmodelElement, which implicitly confines it to the interior of a submodel. The modeling and implementation of events related to the existence and life cycle of an AAS can be challenging, as their possible representation does not necessarily follow the conventional modeling logic typically applied within the AAS framework. This divergence from standard modeling logic may give the impression that the modeling of such events is practically impossible. For example, it raises the question of which submodel could contain an Event element to monitor an AAS that has either not yet been instantiated or has already been removed. The same problem applies to the security, alarm, and infrastructure event types, shown in Fig. 5. This is particularly challenging because such types of events are not currently defined or described within the logic of the AAS metamodel. For example, there is no clear mechanism to reference these events unambiguously within the existing structure, making their integration into the AAS framework non-trivial. This conceptual shortcoming motivates the modeling ap- proaches and potential solution strategies that will be discussed in the following chapters. A. Update of an Existing Submodel Element The first use case illustrates a subscription to changes of a single submodel element. In this use case an AAS designer will be informed if a defined submodel element is changed by an external application (see Fig. 6). In a first step the AAS with the associated submodels is created by the AAS designer. Afterwards, the AAS designer chooses the submodel element which should be observed and gives this information into an Fig. 5. Tree with definition of event types belonging to AAS entities event feature which creates and subscribes the event of type update. When the submodel element, e.g., the value or the metadata like the idShort, is changed (step three), e.g., via an external application, this information will be published from the event feature to the AAS designer who is at the same time the subscriber of the element (step four). The implementation of the event feature will be discussed in Sec. V. Fig. 6. Use Case - Subscription of updates on existing submodel elements B. Creation of a New Submodel This use case demonstrates the subscription of an event which informs when a new submodel in a defined AAS is created (see Fig. 7). This Event element is of type create. The initial situation provides an AAS which can be subscribed by a subscriber through the event feature in a first step. If the AAS designer adds a new submodel to the AAS (step two) the event feature sends the EventPayload to the subscriber, to inform about a new submodel (step three). C. Creation of a New AAS The use case described in this section illustrates a scenario that, as previously mentioned, poses specific challenges for Fig. 7. Use Case - Subscription of newly added submodels in an existing AAS modeling, particularly if the modeling logic of the two earlier examples is followed. In this case, the goal is to subscribe to the creation of a new AAS instance within a known AAS repository. The subscriber wishes to be notified as soon as a new AAS is added. To enable this, the subscriber must first establish a sub- scription to the AAS repository using the event mechanism. When a new AAS is created and uploaded to the repository, the corresponding event is triggered and delivers the EventPayload to the subscriber. However, this raises the question of where the Event element responsible for observing the creation of the new AAS is to be located. In the two previous scenarios, the Event element resided logically within the AAS instance in which the ob- servable change occurred and was tracked. In the present use case, this approach is not feasible, as the AAS being observed does not yet exist at the time the subscription is modeled. D. Conceptual Approach to Address the Issue There are fundamentally two conceptual approaches to address the aforementioned issue: 1) System-Level AAS Representation of the Repository: The first solution involves introducing a dedicated AAS that represents the repository itself, commonly referred to as a System AAS, Environment AAS, or simply the Repository AAS. In this case, the repository is treated as an asset in its own right, and thus a corresponding AAS can be created to describe its internal state. This AAS would include a BasicEventElement configured to observe the repository (i.e., changes in its contents) and notify subscribers when a new AAS is added. This approach gives rise to two follow-up questions: • How should the observation target be modeled? That is, what should be referenced in the observed attribute of the event? • Where should the System AAS itself be located? Possible answers to the second question include: • The repository hosts an AAS instance that describes itself. This AAS is located in the same repository it represents. • Alternatively, the Repository AAS is hosted externally, in a different AAS repository dedicated to infrastructure components. Fig. 8. Use Case - Subscription of newly added AAS in a known repository 2) External Event Infrastructure: Event Element Reposi- tory: A second approach introduces an independent infras- tructure component: a dedicated Event element repository. In analogy to the AAS repository or submodel repositories, this component would manage and host Event elements indepen- dently of specific AAS instances. The hosted Event elements serve as a description for monitoring external behavior (e.g., the creation of an AAS) and therefore support system-level or cross-AAS interactions of other applications. Both approaches highlight current limitations in the AAS specification regarding the modeling of metalevel or life cycle- related events and point to the need for either conceptual extensions or infrastructural enhancements. This contribution focuses on the second approach: the intro- duction of standalone components such as an Event element repository and, by analogy with other AAS concepts, an Event element registry. In this context, a potential adaptation of the Event element definition within the AAS metamodel is dis- cussed. Two key aspects are addressed: the conceptualization of the BasicEventElement as an independent component, rather than strictly as a SubmodelElement, and the minimal adaption required to the current specification of the BasicEventElement, in order to allow the observation of higher-level elements such as repositories in the observed attribute. These three aspects are examined in detail in the following chapters. IV. ADAPTATION OF THE EVENT ELEMENT SPECIFICATION Comparing the current and proposed specification of the BasicEventElement, the major change can be seen in the classes from that the BasicEventElement inherits (see Fig. 10). In order to make as few changes as possible, the inherited classes for the BasicEventElement have been retained, only the class Referable from the SubmodelElement class has changed to the class Identifiable. With this, the meta data of the BasicEventElement can be extended with a globally unique ID and administrative data, like the version of the Event element. The data types of the attributes observed and messageBroker are changed from Referable to Reference. Referable is appropriate for observing SubmodelElements, Fig. 9. Redefined AAS metamodel (based on [8]) Submodels, and AssetAdministrationShells. However, events extend beyond subscribing to changes in values or struc- tural configurations within these objects (as depicted in Fig. 5). Currently, alarms, security violations, and infrastructure events remain unmodeled, underscoring the need for further development in event modeling to encompass these critical aspects. By adjusting the observed attribute to Reference, the scope is expanded, enabling subscriptions to specific repository- or environment-endpoints via the GlobalReference KeyType. This adjustment facilitates comprehensive monitor- ing of repositories and environments as a whole. Opportunities for similar redefinitions exist with regard to the messageBroker attribute. The authors propose to describe the broker with the help of the Asset Interfaces Description submodel [10] because the latest version supports the description of MQTT (Message Queueing Telemetry Transport) and http/REST endpoints. The other attributes of the BasicEventElement are used as described in II-A. The attributes of the EventPayload also remain the same. With the inherited classes, it is necessary to define semanticIds for the different event types of structural changes and updates of existing AAS elements. The tree structure of Fig. 5 serves as a base to define a scheme for creating the semanticId regarding the event type and kind of change that is used. The latter is important because it can be used to validate the EventPayload against the expected data model of the observed element. With the exemplary definition of two event types (structural changes and updates of existing elements), the possible com- binations of kinds of change and AAS elements that can be observed are given in Table I. The first column divides the two types of Event elements. The second column assigns the kinds of changes create, delete, and update to the main event types. The last column maps the AAS elements to the categories of the previous columns. This table can be seen as another kind of view for the tree of Fig. 5. From Table I, the semanticIds can be generated using the Fig. 10. Specification comparison of a) current BasicEventElement and b) redefined BasicEventElement TABLE I DESCRIPTION OF SINGLE ELEMENTS TO GENERATE semanticId event type kind of change AAS element SC (Structural Change) Create AAS Submodel ConceptDescription UEE (Update Existing Element) Delete AAS Update AASMetaInformation ConceptDescription Submodel SubmodelMetaInformation SubmodelElement SubmodelElementMetaInformation proposed scheme: <identifier> ::= <namespace>"/Event/"<event type>"/"<kind of change>"/"<AAS element> <namespace> ::= "https://example.com" <event type> ::= "SC" | "UEE" <kind of change> ::= "Create" | "Update" | "Delete" <AAS element> ::= "AAS" | "AASMetaInformation" | ... (see Table I) As an example, the semanticId for an Event element that observes a submodel element for updates could look like fol- lowing: https://www.example.com/Event/UEE/ Update/SubmodelElement In addition to the mentioned event types, custom events for domain-specific notifications can be defined. These events are identified using a specific semanticId that is globally unique. A software component linked to the corresponding observed AAS element can send or receive these customized events depending on specific conditions, triggers, or behaviors. While the event message format must conform to the defined specification, the payload itself can be entirely customized [8]. An example of a custom event could be the introduction of a new type of product on the market where the EventPayload may include metadata such as product ID and specifications. V. STANDALONE INFRASTRUCTURE COMPONENTS FOR EVENT ELEMENT Following the discussion of the adapted BasicEventElement specification in the previous section, this section focuses on the additional infrastructure components needed to manage an Event element that now stands as a standalone element of the AAS environment. The first subsection briefly describes the available features of the Eclipse BaSyx framework to support the eventing mechanism. Section V-B introduces three key building blocks: an Event element repository for persistent storage, an Event element registry for discovery and routing, and a native event feature within an AAS framework. Further- more, it explains how each one contributes to event handling. As a concrete example, the section outlines the modifications made to the Eclipse BaSyx framework. A. Existing Eventing Technologies in Eclipse BaSyx Currently, frameworks such as Eclipse BaSyx support MQTT eventing mechanisms to be informed when changes in an AAS repository 2 or event submodel repository 3 occur. The AAS repository publishes topics about the creation, update, or deletion of an AAS as well as the creation and deletion of a submodel reference in an AAS. The submodel repository does the same for submodels as well for submodel elements and especially for file elements, i.e. when a file is updated or deleted. Eclipse BaSyx also offers an eventing mechanism that informs the subscriber that the element changed but does not include the element and thus the value itself. However, the events are not described by the specified AAS Event elements. This direct, technology-specific implementation appears to lack the semantic layer that the Event element within the AAS metamodel provides. The Event element is intended to allow a formalized and machine-interpretable description of the Event element’s structure, context, and its linkage to observable ele- ments. This abstraction should enable technology-independent modeling, support interoperability across heterogeneous sys- tems, and ensure that events are not merely transmitted, but also meaningfully understood within the AAS ecosystem. B. Concept for New Infrastructure Components Figure 11 introduces the Event element repository and Event element registry, which implementation is based on the existing submodel repository and submodel registry compo- nents, respectively. These concepts rely on the functionality of existing AAS infrastructure components. Therefore, a potential implementation or adaptation could be realized with minimal additional effort. 2AAS repository MQTT eventing feature 3Submodel repository MQTT eventing feature Fig. 11. Concept for AAS Event element infrastructure TABLE II INTERFACE SPECIFICATION OF EVENT ELEMENT REPOSITORY Operation Description GetAllEventElements Returns all Event elements GetEventElementById Returns a specific Event element GetAllEventElementsBy SemanticId Returns all Event elements with a specific semanticId GetAllEventElementsBy IdShort Returns all Event elements with a specific idShort PostEventElement Creates a new Event element. The ID of the new Event element must be set in the payload. PutEventElementById Replaces an existing Event element DeleteEventElementById Deletes an Event element 1) Event Element Repository: The Event element repos- itory serves as the central element of the new AAS Event element infrastructure by hosting BasicEventElements. The operations for managing and manipulating these elements are derived from those used in the submodel repository interface specification [2] but have been adapted specifically for Event elements (see Table II). Additionally, the repository functions as an endpoint provider for external messaging infrastructures, for example, the event feature (see Sec. V-B3). This separation introduces advantages, such as decoupling Event element storage from real-time delivery logic, but creates a dependency on external systems to handle Event elements and distribution. This decoupling means that Event elements can be described independently of the underlying implementation technology. A key design trade-off concerns subscriber management. Instead of managing a central data base of subscriptions inside the event feature, the alternative is to rely on message brokers (like in the proposed event feature), with which their sub- scribers can interact directly. This preserves simplicity, but risks fragmentation if different subscribers use incompatible protocols. 2) Event Element Registry: To manage the access to the BasicEventElements an Event element registry is specified. Like the submodel registry it manages EventDescriptors that provide endpoints and meta data for their associated BasicEventElements. The EventDescriptor currently does not exist in the AAS metamodel, so that it was specified by the au- thors. To facilitate functional consistency, the EventDescriptor (see Fig. 12) is defined with attributes mirroring those of TABLE III INTERFACE SPECIFICATION OF EVENT ELEMENT REGISTRY Operation Description GetAllEventDescriptors Returns all Event Descriptors GetEventDescriptorById Returns a specific Event Descriptor PostEventDescriptor Creates a new Event Descriptor, i.e. reg- isters an Event PutEventDescriptorById Replaces an existing Event Descriptor, i.e., replaces registration information DeleteEventDescriptorById Deletes an Event Descriptor, i.e., de- registers an Event element Fig. 12. The specification of the new EventDescriptor (based on [2]) the SubmodelDescriptor. The registry’s interface operations include the one in Table III, which are adapted similarly from the submodel registry interface specification [2]. 3) Event Feature: The two presented infrastructure com- ponents, Event element repository and Event element reg- istry, only store the descriptions of Event elements without implementing logic to distribute EventPayloads. To enable the exchange of EventPayloads an eventing feature must be developed to observe changes in a given AAS repository regarding AAS Event elements and to distribute the published EventPayload to subscribed applications. For this, an architec- ture concept is shown in Fig. 13. The AAS event feature contains an orchestration layer which configures and manages the components inside the feature. The Event element repository stores all BasicEventElements and is used to configure the event payload transformer and an optional event router. The transformer receives the EventPayloads from an AAS repository observer outside of the event feature. This observer uses the AAS repository as the event source and overtakes the role of the publisher and publishes changes through non-standardized events to the event payload transformer inside the event feature. The observer could be, for example, the Eclipse BaSyx MQTT feature (see Sec. V-A). The transformer then converts the proprietary event payload into the specified AAS EventPayload format and forwards it to the event router that configures and distributes the EventPayloads to several message brokers. A subscriber can ask through the API of the event feature for the BasicEventElement to get the endpoint description of the belonging message broker and connects directly to it for message transfer. The event feature offers a promising hybrid approach by standardizing interfaces for external messaging systems to interact with the Event element repository. Future work must Fig. 13. Concept of the event feature show if this concept enables decoupled scalability and in- teroperability, as it allows leveraging existing infrastructure like MQTT, AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) or Kafka for delivery while retaining centralized storage of BasicEventElements in the Event element repository. Advan- tages include flexibility and alignment with industry standards, but challenges persist: latency in cross-component coordina- tion could hinder real-time applications, and semantic incon- sistencies may arise if publishers or subscribers interpret Event elements differently without rigorous identifier standardization (e.g., semanticId). Overall, while the framework improves the management of AAS Event elements through modular design and standardized interfaces, its success depends on eliminating these architectural ambiguities and striking a balance between centralization and interoperability. C. Application of the Presented Approach to Event Payloads Following the logic of the presented approach, the question arises whether it would be reasonable to apply a similar repository/registry pattern to EventPayloads themselves. The next phase of research could investigate whether such a concept would offer added value, e.g., by enabling the reuse, classification, or standardized referencing of payload structures and what specific advantages such an extension might provide within the broader AAS ecosystem. VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK This paper contributes to a deeper understanding of the po- tential applications of the BasicEventElement within the AAS and serves as a logical continuation of the ongoing publication series dedicated to explaining the capabilities of the AAS event mechanism. Within this series, the AAS specification defines the structural foundation, the AAS Events white paper [9] provides conceptual context, and the present work focuses on the concrete modeling and implementation aspects of events related to the existence and lifecycle of AAS instances and their environments, a category of events that is anticipated in the AAS metamodel but not yet formally specified. The paper identifies central challenges associated with rep- resenting such events, formulates key questions that modelers and system architects may encounter, and outlines a potential solution space. One specific modeling approach, based on the introduction of standalone infrastructure components such as an Event element repository and Event element registry, is described in greater detail as a conceptual building block towards a consistent, event-driven architecture grounded in the AAS paradigm. At the same time, this paper highlights that further sys- tematic investigation is necessary to fully explore the model- ing, technical, and semantic dimensions of AAS-based event handling. Future work should aim to consolidate existing ap- proaches, evaluate their respective advantages and limitations, and develop a reference architecture for event-driven AAS sys- tems. Such an architecture should include characteristic design patterns, integration strategies, and decision-making guidelines to support practitioners in implementing and embedding event- driven mechanisms into their system landscapes and product architectures.
